bids.reasoning: The negotiation has just begun, and the opponent has opened with the lowest possible utility for me across all issues, attempting to justify it with unverified claims about industry standards. Since it is early in the negotiation (t=0.0), I will anchor firmly at my maximum utility choice to set a strong baseline. I will bid `valueA` across all issues, which maximizes my total utility to 1.0.

Early in the negotiation (t=0.02), the opponent has made consecutive identical offers with the lowest possible utility for me, using deceptive constraint tactics ("market rate" and "director's cap") to pressure me. I will ignore their unverified claims and make a very minor concession on my lowest-importance issue (issueF to valueB) to signal a willingness to negotiate, while maintaining a very high total utility of roughly 0.96.

The current time is very early in the negotiation (t=0.04), and the opponent has repeated their minimum-utility opening offer of all "valueC" claims while using generic psychological pressure tactics in their messages. Since their assertions of "capped authority" and "industry standard" are unsupported cheap talk, I will ignore them and continue to prioritize my own utility. I will make a small concession on another low-weighted issue (issueE) to signal a willingness to negotiate, while holding firm on my most important issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC).

The current time is very early (t=0.06), so I do not need to make drastic concessions. The opponent is using repetitive cheap talk, falsely claiming to have reached a hard limit in the very first rounds while repeatedly offering a package that gives me minimum utility. I will proceed with gradual concessions on my least important issues (dropping issueD to valueB) while maintaining valueA for my highest priority issues (A, B, and C) to invite a genuine counter-offer when the opponent is ready to negotiate.

The current time fraction is very low (t=0.08), indicating we are still in the early stages of negotiation. My opponent has made a slight concession on issueB (from valueC to valueB), relying on cheap-talk tactics about hard limits. I will reciprocate their small concession by lowering my demand on issueF (my least important issue, weight 0.1) to valueC, while holding firm on my highest-weight issues (issueA, issueB, issueC) to maintain a high utility.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.10), giving me plenty of time to explore compromises. The opponent has made a minor concession on issueB but continues to heavily anchor on terms highly unfavorable to me, accompanied by cheap talk about "strict mandates." To reciprocate their slight movement without sacrificing my core interests, I will concede on issueE, which holds lower weight for me. I will maintain a firm stance on my high-priority issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to maximize my utility.

I am targeting a utility of around 0.80. Since time pressure is very low (t=0.12), I will maintain my positions on my most valuable issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to secure the bulk of my utility. The opponent's message history is repetitive and consists of cheap talk regarding inflexible mandates, which contradicts their slight concession on issueB; I will therefore rely on their actual offers rather than their messages. I am swapping values among the lower-weighted issues (issueD, issueE, issueF) to explore possible mutually acceptable combinations without decreasing my own overall utility.

The time fraction is still low (0.14), so I have plenty of time to negotiate while optimizing for my own utility. The opponent's message remains cheap talk relying on a fabricated limit, but they have started making small concessions, offering valueB on issueA. Since issueA, issueB, and issueC are my highest impact issues (totaling 0.7 weight), I will hold them firmly at valueA. I will concede issueD, issueE, and issueF completely to valueC to see if this trade-off encourages the opponent to continue moving on my main priorities.

The opponent is consistently making minimal concessions, rotating a single 'valueB' offer across issueB, issueA, and now issueD while using repetitive standard-text messages about having capped authorization. Since we are still very early in the negotiation (t=0.16), I will ignore their exaggerated messages and continue to hold firm on my most valuable issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC). I will match their concession on issueD by offering 'valueB' there, recovering some utility on issueE and issueF that I conceded too early in previous rounds.

The opponent is testing individual concessions by offering `valueB` on `issueB`, `issueA`, `issueD`, and now `issueC` across subsequent rounds, despite their messages repeatedly claiming they have hit a hard limit. Because it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.18), I will stand firm on my most highly weighted issues (`issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueC`) while providing minor variations in concessions on my lower-weighted issues (`issueD`, `issueE`, and `issueF`) to encourage further movement from them.

The current time is 0.2, still early in the negotiation. The opponent's messages are repetitive cheap talk, but their offers show concessions on issues A, B, C, and D, with their latest offering my top value for issueB, which is great for me. I will anchor on valueA for my highest-weight issues (A, B, C) to ensure high utility (target ~0.84), while conceding on lower-priority issues (D, E, F) by providing valueB or valueC to keep exploring mutual trade-offs.

The opponent has repeatedly used boilerplate messages claiming zero authorization flexibility, yet their actual bidding history shows progressive compromises, particularly recently conceding issueB to valueA. Since issueB has a high importance for me, I will gladly accept this concession by including it in my counteroffer. To reciprocate while maintaining a high utility early in the game (t=0.22), I will concede on my lower-weight issues (D and F) while holding firm on the highest-weight items (A and C).

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive cheap talk about absolute caps, but their actual bidding behavior reveals significant flexibility, culminating in their latest concession on my most valuable issue (`issueA: valueA`). I will strategically lock in `valueA` for my top-weight issues (`issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueC`) while offering total concessions on minor issues (`issueD` and `issueF` to `valueC`) to test their constraints without significantly sacrificing my utility. Since we are still early in the negotiation at t=0.24, I can afford to maintain a high-utility threshold.

The current time fraction is 0.26, meaning it is still relatively early in the negotiation. The opponent's messages are repetitive declarations of limits that change dynamically, indicating a bluff, and they have occasionally offered favorable values on my high-priority issues (like `issueA` to `valueA` and `issueB` to `valueA`). I will maintain a high-utility demand for my highest priorities (`issueA`, `issueB`, `issueC`) while offering different concession permutations on the minor issues (`issueD`, `issueE`, `issueF`) to find what might be acceptable.

The negotiation is still in the early-to-mid stage (t=0.28). The opponent's messages rely heavily on identical scripted responses, so rather than trusting their stated authorization limits, I am observing their bidding behavior, which shows them gradually experimenting with concessions on `issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueC`. I will hold completely firm on my most highly valued issues (`A`, `B`, and `C`) and offer minor concessions on my less critical issues (`D`, `E`, and `F`) to explore their acceptable trade-offs while maintaining a high utility (>0.84).

The current time is 0.30, which is still early in the negotiation. The opponent's messaging history is composed heavily of cheap talk, repeatedly claiming to be at their absolute limit since t=0.02 while continuously making minor concessions. In their most recent bid, they offered 'valueB' for issueB and issueE; I will reciprocate by including these values since they retain strong utility for me, while maintaining 'valueA' on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueC).

The opponent's messages consist matching boilerplate claims of hard constraints, which I interpret as cheap talk, especially since their offers are shifting (recently conceding to valueA on issueA and valueB on issueB). I am maintaining a target utility of ~0.76 by matching their offer of valueB on issueB and securing my top preference (valueA) on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueC). To show flexibility, I will propose further mid-level concessions on the lower-weight issues (issueD, issueE, and issueF).

The opponent's messages consist of a repeated sequence of fixed "hard limit" claims that directly contradict their shifting bids, demonstrating that these messages are just cheap talk. The opponent has recently offered `valueA` for `issueA` and `valueB` for `issueB` and `issueF`. I will target a utility of approximately 0.8 by anchoring on these revealed concessions while maintaining my top values on other important issues to leave room for further negotiation.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive, fabricated constraints and fake limits, which I will disregard as cheap talk since their bidding history shows a willingness to concede. To keep negotiations moving at this early-mid stage (t=0.36), I am targeting a utility of approx 0.80 by maintaining my top values on high-weight issues (issueA and issueC). For the remaining issues, I am integrating the opponent's past concessions by offering 'valueB', which they have demonstrated a willingness to accept on issues B, D, E, and F.

The opponent's messages are clearly using a fake constraint tactic, claiming they've hit a hard limit, yet their offers keep changing. Specifically, in their last offer, they conceded `issueB` to `valueA` and `issueE` to `valueB`, revealing they actually have room to move. At this stage (t=0.38), I will accept these values and maintain a firm stance on my high-priority issues (`issueA`, `issueB`, `issueC`) while offering a slight concession on the lowest priority issues to encourage further movement.

The opponent's messages are purely tactical and utilize fabricated constraints, so I will ignore them and focus on their bidding behavior. Recently, they have shown a willingness to concede by offering 'valueA' for 'issueE', and previously for 'issueA' and 'issueB'. As we approach mid-game at t=0.4, I will capitalize on these signals by holding firm on my high-weight items ('issueA', 'issueB', 'issueC', and 'issueE') with my top choices. To show good faith and drive towards an agreement, I will offer a deeper concession on 'issueD', a low-importance issue for me.

The current time is roughly halfway to the deadline. I should maintain a firm stance on my most important issue (issueA) for which the opponent has already demonstrated willingness to accept 'valueA'. I will concede slightly on issueC to 'valueB' to match their earlier offers at t=0.18 and t=0.28, and accept their recent moves on issueB ('valueA') and issueF ('valueB'). I will also offer 'valueA' for issueE, matching their proposal from t=0.40, while conceding on issueD to their preferred 'valueC'. The persistent "absolute limit" messages are treated as standard cheap talk since their strict limit keeps shifting.

The opponent's messages are repetitive, fabricated claims about authorization limits, yet their bidding behavior consistently explores various tradeoffs, including offering `valueA` for `issueD` and `valueB` for `issueC` in their latest bid. I will ignore their cheap talk about reservation values. To target a utility around 0.80, I will leverage their recent concession on `issueD` while holding firm on my most highly-weighted issues (`issueA` and `issueB`), offering them their preferred `valueC` on `issueE` to keep the process moving toward agreement.

Although the opponent repeatedly claims they have reached an absolute boundary mandated by their principal, their bidding history proves otherwise as they continue to make concessions across multiple issues (offering `valueA` for `issueB` and `valueB` for `issueF`). I will ignore their cheap talk and focus on matching their pattern of partial concessions. I am holding firm on my high-priority issues (`issueA` and `issueB`), while offering `valueB` across the remaining issues to find an equitable middle ground, maintaining a solid utility around 0.80 to keep pressure on before the deadline.

At time 0.48, the opponent offered valueA for issueA and valueB for issues C and D, which aligns with my priorities and demonstrates some willingness to concede on key issues despite their messages claiming a strict cap. I will reciprocate by adopting their recent proposals for issues C and D, while holding firm on issues A and B where my weights are highest. I will also concede on issue E to explore tradeoffs that might satisfy their unstated preferences, aiming for a favorable mid-game package.

The opponent's statements claiming they have hit their "absolute reservation value" have been repeated constantly since t=0.00, contradicting their ongoing variations in offers, so I'll treat them as cheap talk. Since we are exactly at the halfway mark (t=0.5), I aim to hold my most important issues (issueA and issueB) at valueA, as the opponent has shown willingness to concede on these (e.g., issueA: valueA at t=0.48, issueB: valueA at t=0.50). I will incorporate their previous concessions on issueD (valueA at t=0.44) and issueC (valueB at t=0.48) to keep my utility above 0.8 while presenting a new permutation they might accept.

I am targeting a utility of around 0.76 by combining the opponent's recent concessions on specific issues. Despite the opponent's cheap talk claiming that they keep hitting their "absolute reservation value", their bids continuously shift, showing they still have flexibility. I will hold firm on my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB at valueA) while accepting their recent offers of valueB on issueC, valueA on issueD, and valueC on issues E and F.

I am approaching the midway point (t=0.54) and observing the opponent's negotiation patterns despite their repetitive messages about hard limits. I am maintaining my most critical issues, `issueA` and `issueB`, at my top value (`valueA`), as the opponent has previously accepted them in separate offers. To encourage agreement, I am matching several of their recent concessions, including `issueC: valueB` and `issueE: valueB`, while accepting their demand for `issueF: valueC`.

I am ignoring the opponent's repetitive messages citing "principal authorization" and "reservation value limits" as they contradict their actual bidding history, which shows high flexibility. Since the opponent just offered valueA for issueB (my 2nd highest weight) and valueB for issueE and issueF, I will embrace those values. I will maintain issueA at valueA to secure my highest-utility item, while offering a unified suite of valueB for issues C, D, E, and F, maintaining my utility at a strong ~0.80 while extending an objectively flexible package based on their recent moves.

The opponent is relying heavily on cheap talk, repeatedly claiming they are at their authorized limit while continually changing their offers. Thus, I will ignore their messages and track their actual bids, which show potential flexibility on issues E, D, and C. Since we are around t=0.58, I will maintain my top values on my highest-weight issues (A and B) while adopting their recently proposed values on issues E (valueA) and D (valueC) to present a mutually beneficial tradeoff, preserving an overall utility around 0.80.

The opponent continues to rely heavily on standard negotiation tactics, specifically fake constraints and fabricated ceilings, which contradict the variation in their historical bids. At `t=0.6`, I am looking to compromise on issues of lower importance (such as issueC, issueD, issueE) while holding firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueB). Since the opponent just included `valueA` for `issueB` and `issueF` in their last turn, I will integrate those exact values along with some previous values they have proposed (`issueC: valueB`, `issueD: valueB`, `issueE: valueB`) to craft an offer that aligns with both our interests, maintaining a high utility of roughly 0.84.

The opponent's messages consist uniformly of fake authorization constraints, which contradict their varied bidding behavior, so I will strictly follow the numerical signals from their offers. In the latest round, they made significant concessions on issueC (valueA) and issueD (valueA). I will incorporate these concessions while maintaining my demand for issueA and issueB (where they have previously shown flexibility), and concede issueE and issueF entirely to valueC to find a mutually agreeable package while keeping my utility above 0.80.

The opponent's messaging remains aggressive with rigid but demonstrably expanding boundaries, as their offers show increasing flexibility on issues like C, D, and E. Since we are at t=0.64, I will adopt the values the opponent just proposed for issues C, D, E, and F, as they still yield a sufficiently high overall utility for me, while standing firm on my two most crucial issues (A and B) by keeping them at my top preference. This signals a willingness to coordinate without sacrificing my major sources of utility.

I am targeting a utility around 0.80 as we enter the later stages of the negotiation. The opponent's natural language messages are completely rigid, but their actual offers show movement, recently conceding to `valueA` on `issueF` and `valueB` on `issueB`. Taking these signals into account, I will match their concessions on `issueB` and `issueF` while holding firm on my most valuable issue (`issueA`) and other key high-utility issues to construct a mutually beneficial compromise.

The current time is 0.68, and the deadline is approaching. The opponent's last offer (A=valueA, C=valueA, D=valueA, B=valueC, E=valueC, F=valueC) tells me that they are willing to concede on issues A, C, and D to secure their preferred values on B, E, and F. Since my weights for E and F are low (0.1 each), I am ready to concede those to their preferred valueC. However, since my weight for issueB is high (0.2), I will counter by compromising on issueB with valueB while keeping A, C, and D at valueA.

I am targeting a utility around 0.8 by securing my most valued terms on issueA and issueC. At t=0.7, ignoring the opponent's non-verifiable claims of rigid authorization limits, I note they have recently proposed configurations on issueD, issueE, and issueF that coincidentally provide me with fair utility. I will adopt their most recent values for those three lower-weight issues while offering a mid-tier compromise on issueB and holding firm on issueC, allowing me to concede where it costs me less.

The negotiation revolves around securing high-utility values on issues A, B, and C, where my importance weights are the highest. The opponent's message history is a persistent stream of false hard-limits regarding their authorization and the industry standard, which I will ignore since their mathematical sequence of concessions tells a different story. I am dropping down to a utility around 0.80 by piecing together components they have individually offered recently (e.g., A=A and C=A at t=0.68, B=B at t=0.72), extending concessions on lesser-weighted issues E and F.

The opponent's messages constantly claim they cannot go lower, yet their latest offer at t=0.74 significantly shifts to include valueA for issueB and valueB for several other issues, contradicting their "hard limit" assertions. Since time fraction is 0.74, I will maintain my top priority (issueA: valueA) while accepting their most recent concessions on issueB, issueC, issueD, and issueE (valueA, valueB, valueB, and valueB, respectively) and pairing it with valueC on issueF which they historical favor, yielding me a strong utility around 0.76.
